mcq.question_md: A page built entirely with `<div>` tags looks pixel-for-pixel identical to one using `<header>`, `<nav>`, and `<main>`. So why do experienced developers still insist on the semantic version?
options: Semantic tags come with built-in styling, so the page needs less CSS
Browsers render semantic tags faster than divs
They carry meaning — screen readers, search engines, and other developers understand the page's structure
Divs are deprecated and will stop working in future browsers
mcq.explanation_md: Semantic tags are about *meaning*, not looks: a screen reader can jump a blind user straight to `<main>`, search engines weigh content by its structural role, and a teammate reading `<nav>` knows instantly what they're looking at. The built-in-styling option is the tempting one — a few semantic tags do have minor default styles — but that's incidental, never the reason, and you override defaults anyway. Rendering speed is identical, and `<div>` is not deprecated; it's still the right tool for purely visual grouping that has no meaning to convey.
